segments.text:
1.1 Chemistry—The Science of Everyday Experience
Chemistry is the study of matter—its composition,
properties, and transformations.
Matter is anything that has mass and takes up volume.
Matter can be: naturally occurring, or synthetic materials
Would you be
surprised to know
that all of these
synthetic materials
come from
petroleum?!
What states of matter do you see in this picture of Mendenhall Glacier, in Juneau, Alaska?

The Solid State:
A solid has a definite volume .
It maintains its shape regardless of its container.
Solid particles lie close together in a regular pattern.
Solids cannot be compressed .
1.2 The States of Matter

The Liquid State:
A liquid has a definite volume .
It takes the shape of its container .
Liquid particles are close together but can move past one another.
Liquids cannot be compressed .

The Gas State:
A gas has no definite shape ; it assumes the shape of its container.
It has no definite volume ; it assumes the volume of its container.
Gas particles are very far apart and move around randomly .
Gases can be compressed !

Physical properties can be observed or measured
without changing the composition of the material.
• boiling point
melting point
solubility
crystalline shape
• color
odor
state of matter
density

Chemical properties determine how a substance can be converted into another substance.
Chemical change is the chemical reaction that converts one substance into another.
Reactants                          Products

A pure substance is composed of only a single component (atom or molecule).
It has a constant composition , regardless of sample size or origin of sample.
It cannot be broken down to other pure substances by a physical change.
1.3  Classification of Matter
All matter can be classified as either a pure substance or a mixture.
Table sugar (C 12 H 22 O 11 ) and water (H 2 O) are both
pure substances:

Mixtures are composed of more than one component .
They can have varying composition (any combination of solid, liquid, and gas).
Mixtures can be separated into their components by a physical process .
Sugar dissolved in water is a mixture.

An element is a pure substance that cannot be broken down by a chemical change.
A pure substance is classified as an element or a compound .
Composed of atoms 
 Composed
of molecules
A compound is a pure substance formed by chemically joining two or more elements.

Try problems 1.4, 1.5 and 1.6 found on page 8 in your text. The answers to sample problems may be found in your text, and at the end of the chapter.  Appendix D has helpful information about computations and calculators.

1.4 Measurement
The number is meaningless without the unit.
proper aspirin dosage = 325 (milligrams or pounds?)
a fast time for the 100-meter dash = 10.00 (seconds or days?)
Every measurement is composed of a number and a unit .
Examples:
Each type of measurement has a base unit in the metric system. .

Other units are related to the base unit by a power of 10 .
The prefix of the unit name indicates if the unit is larger or smaller than the base unit.

1 kilo meter (km) = 1,000 meters (m)
1 km = 1,000 m
1 milli meter (mm) = 0.001 meters (m)
1 mm = 0.001 m
or
1000 mm = 1 m
1 centi meter (cm) = 0.01 meters (m)
1 cm = 0.01 m
or
100 cm = 1 m
The base unit of length is the meter (m).

Mass is a measure of the amount of matter in an object.
Weight is the force that matter feels due to gravity.
( weight = mass x gravity )
1 kilo gram (kg) = 1,000 grams (g)
1 kg = 1,000 g
1 milli gram (mg) = 0.001 grams (g)
1 mg = 0.001 g
or
1000 mg = 1 g
The base unit of mass is the gram (g).

1 kilo liter ( kL ) = 1,000 liters (L)
1 kL = 1,000 L
1 milli liter (mL) = 0.001 liters (L)
1 mL = 0.001 L
or
1000 mL = 1 L
Volume = Length x Width x Height
= cm x cm x cm
= cm 3
1 mL = 1 cm 3 = 1 cc
100 mL = 1 dL or deciliter
The base unit of volume is the liter (L).

An exact number results from counting objects or is
part of a definition.
10 fingers
10 people
1 meter = 100 centimeters
An inexact number results from a measurement or
observation and contains some uncertainty.
15.3 cm
1000.8 g
0.0034 mL
1.5 Significant Figures

Significant figures are all the digits in a measured
number including one estimated digit.
All nonzero digits are always significant.
3 sig. figures
6 sig. figures
65 . 2 g
255 . 345 g
65.2 g
255.345 g

3 sig. figures
Rule 1 : A zero counts as a significant figure when
it occurs:
between two nonzero digits
5 sig. figures
at the end of a number with a decimal point

1.7 Problem Solving Using Conversion Factors
Conversion factor : A term that converts a quantity in
one unit to a quantity in another unit.
Conversion factors are usually written as equalities .
2.20 lb = 1 kg
To use them, they must be written as ratios .
original
quantity
conversion factor
desired
quantity
x
=
2.20 lb
1 kg
or
1 kg
2.20 lb

If a unit appears in the numerator in one term and the denominator in another term , the units cancel .
The goal in setting up a problem is to make sure all unwanted units cancel .
To convert 130 lb into kilograms:
130 lb

The unwanted unit lb cancels.
The desired unit kg does not cancel.

Always arrange the units so that the denominator in
one term cancels the numerator in the preceding term.

2 pints = 1 quart

1.9 Temperature
Temperature is a measure of how hot or cold an object is.  Particles in a hot object move at a
higher speed than particles in a cold object.
Degrees Fahrenheit ( o F )
Degrees Celsius ( o C )
Kelvin (K)
Three temperature scales are used:
To convert from o C to o F:
To convert from o F to o C:
o F =  1.8( o C )  +  32
o C  = o F − 32
1.8
To convert from o C to K:
K  = o C +  273
o C  =  K − 273
To convert from K to o C:

Comparing the Three Temperature Scales
5(212 – 32) = 100
9

1.10 Density and Specific Gravity A. Density
density
=
mass (g)
volume (mL or cc)
Density : A physical property that relates the mass of
a substance to its volume.

If the density of acetic acid is 1.05 g/mL, what is
the volume of 5.0 grams of acetic acid?
5.0 g
original quantity

desired quantity

For most substances, the solid state is denser than the liquid state.
Water is an exception!
When liquid water freezes to become solid ice,
it expands .
What impact will this have on the:
volume of the water sample?
on the density of the water sample?
D = M
V
Water is densest at 4 ºC and defined as 1.00 g/mL .

Specific Gravity
Specific gravity : A quantity that compares the density
of a substance with the density of water at 4 ºC.
specific gravity
=
density of a substance (g/mL)
density of water (g/mL)
The units of the numerator (g/mL) cancel the
units of the denominator (g/mL).
The specific gravity of a substance is equal to its
density, but contains no units .
